The case involves an appeal against a decision by the Athens Tripartite Board of Assessors (Τριμελές Εφετείο Αθηνών) regarding a fraud conviction. The key points are: 1. The Athens Tripartite Board did not examine whether the accused individuals were indeed owners of certain properties, as this falls under the jurisdiction of civil courts. 2. While this decision was within their legal authority, it failed to address the civil ownership issue for the convicted individuals. 3. This omission led to a claim that the decision violated Article 510 para. 1 sect. Η' of the Penal Code (Κώδικα Ποινικών Δικαιωμάτων). 4. The appeal court rejected this argument, finding no other grounds for annulment. 5. As a result, it dismissed the appeal against the Athens Tripartite Board's decision on June 16, 2017. The summary concludes with details of when and where the judgment was published and signed by the Vice President and Secretary.

The key points of this decision are: - The appeal for commutation by the convicted A. P. was rejected as it did not provide any valid grounds for commutation. - The reasons given in support of the appeal were deemed vague and unacceptable, particularly those related to the assessment of evidence and requests for leniency from the Areios Pagos (Supreme Court). - As a result, the appeal was found to be invalid. - The appellant (A. P.) was sentenced to pay 250 euros in judicial fees for the criminal proceedings and 500 euros in public interest legal costs. - This decision was rendered on June 7, 2017 in Athens by the Vice President of the Court, with a senior Areopagite also present. - The decision was published publicly on September 18, 2018 at the court building in Athens. The main issue was that the appeal did not provide sufficient or specific grounds for commutation as required by law.

This decision is a court ruling rejecting an appeal to dismiss a criminal case against a defendant named A. R., who was accused of attempting to bribe the judges in a trial related to a lawsuit (case number 29591/2016). The key points are: 1. The appeal to dismiss the case was rejected. 2. The defendant, A. R., is ordered to pay court costs amounting to €250. 3. He is also sentenced to pay a fine of €500 for politically motivated bribery. The decision details several reasons why the appeal should be dismissed: - One piece of evidence (a document) was considered by the court without being read aloud during the trial, which would normally invalidate the proceedings. However, in this case, it only addressed whether there was legitimate authority to sign and initiate a complaint against the defendant, not his guilt. - There were no other valid grounds for dismissing the appeal. The decision was made on April 5, 2017, in Athens, Greece. It was signed by the Vice President of the court (Antiprōdos H Grammatēs) and published publicly at an open session in Athens on September 28, 2018

The case was about a dispute over holiday pay. The first instance decision (359/2016) by the Tripartite Chamber of Piraeus was annulled due to several reasons: 1. The court found that the decision violated the principles applied, as it used an inconsistent approach in determining the holiday pay. 2. Specifically, the court argued that the first instance did not properly apply the criteria for calculating the holiday pay based on "performance" and "more favorable employment." 3. The court ruled that the case should be retried by a different panel of judges from those who issued the original decision. As a result: 1. The 359/2016 decision was annulled. 2. The case was referred back to the same Tripartite Chamber, but with a new panel of judges. 3. The losing party (the one that filed the appeal) was ordered to pay approximately 2.3 million euros in legal fees. The decision was made on April 17, 2018, and published in an official session at the court in Athens on May 22, 2018. The summary is signed by the Vice President and Secretary of the court.

This summary appears to be a legal decision regarding an appeal against a judgment. Here's a breakdown of the key points: 1. The court rejected an appeal filed on April 27, 2017, challenging a decision from the Single Chamber of Larissa. 2. The appellant (referred to as "the defendant" in Greek legal terms) was found guilty and ordered to pay 1,700 euros in legal fees. 3. This decision was made on April 24, 2018. 4. The document states that the judgment was publicly announced at a session held in Athens on May 22, 2018. 5. It mentions specific roles: "Prosecretary" and "Secretary General". The summary is written in Greek and appears to be an official court decision or judgment.

The key points of this decision are: - The court rejected an appeal from I. X. N., dated October 19, 2015, seeking to overturn a decision by the Three-member Plenum Court of Athens (dated October 20, 2015) in case number 30662/2015. - The court found that there was no valid reason for overturning the original decision. Specifically: - The appeal lacked detailed information about the witness's whereabouts and address - The court did not have a duty to respond or provide reasons for rejecting this vague request - The court that issued the final decision had already rejected an earlier similar request without success - As a result, the appeal was dismissed. - The appellant (I. X. N.) will be ordered to pay judicial costs of 250 euros. The decision was rendered in Athens on May 17, 2017 and published publicly at an open session in the court building on May 9, 2018.

The summary of the court decision is as follows: - The case was split for seven individuals (Γ. Ε., Ζ. Σ., Κ. Δ., Κ. Μ., Σ. Μ., Σ. Π., Κ. Σ.) - The discussion regarding these cases was deemed unacceptable. - The appeal from the 6th of July 2016, with case number ..., was rejected. - The judgment against the municipality (ΝΠΔΔ) was condemned to pay 900 euros in half of the judicial costs. - The decision was made on January 16, 2018, in Athens. - It was published publicly at a public session in the court's auditorium in Athens on February 13, 2018. The reasons for this decision include: - Rejection of the appeal due to lack of grounds - Unacceptable content of the discussion regarding the cases - The municipality's appeal was rejected as it did not provide valid grounds for the claim - The court found that the claims were not supported by the facts as evaluated by the trial court, which are generally unreviewable The decision specifically addresses a case where individuals appealed an earlier judgment

This summary covers the key points of a court decision rejecting an appeal and ordering the losing party to pay legal fees: 1. The decision rejects an appeal filed by an anonymous company on September 29, 2015 against a ruling from the Thessaloniki Ephorie (local administrative body). 2. It orders the appeal to be dismissed. 3. It directs that the case file be submitted to the State Treasury. 4. It sentences the losing party (the appealing company) to pay legal fees of 2,700 euros to the winning party. 5. The decision was made in Athens on June 15, 2017 and signed by both the Vice President and Secretary. 6. It was published publicly at a session held in Athens on December 18, 2017. The summary does not include details of the case itself or the specific reasons for the ruling, focusing instead on the procedural aspects of the decision.

Here's a summary of the key points from the court decision: - The case concerns an appeal to rescind (anairesis) a previous decision by the Heraklion Mixed Court. - The appellant (Α. Σ. του Ι.) failed to appear in person or through counsel for the initial hearing on March 15, 2017. - An attempt was made to summon the appellant via a notice dated February 18, 2017, but he did not attend when called and did not present himself at the court reading of the decision on March 15, 2017. - The appeal was submitted in writing on January 13, 2017 with an accompanying submission (ekthesis). - The court rejected both the request for adjournment and the appeal to rescind the previous decision. - The appellant was ordered to pay 250 euros in legal fees. The court found that by not appearing as required, the appellant forfeited his right to contest the original decision.

This decision rejects an appeal against a conviction for fraudulent appropriation under Article 375(1) of the Greek Penal Code. The key points are: - The appeal was filed by lawyer G. T. on behalf of E., challenging the 117/2015 judgment. - The court rejected the appeal, finding no valid grounds for annulment. - The court ordered the appellant to pay 250 euros in legal fees for the criminal proceedings. - The appellant was also ordered to pay 500 euros in costs to the state. - E. was originally convicted of fraudulent appropriation on October 24, 2010. - The decision was publicly announced at a hearing in Athens on September 11, 2017. The court found no merit in the appeal arguments based on alleged improper interpretation and application of an essential criminal provision, as well as any overreach by the court. It upheld the original conviction.

This decision rejects an appeal for the release of a judgment from March 4, 2016, brought by D. P. and A. against the 518/2015 decision of the Thessaloniki Mixed Epheta (a court in Greece). The reasons given are: 1. The appellant was legally and promptly summoned to appear but did not show up or have a lawyer represent them. 2. Since there has been no improper application of a later, more lenient law that came into effect after the original judgment, and given these circumstances, the appeal should be rejected. 3. As a result, the appellant will be ordered to pay 250 euros in legal fees for the criminal proceedings. The decision was rendered on December 21, 2016, in Athens, and published publicly at an open session of the court on September 7, 2017. Key points: - Appellant failed to appear as summoned - No improper application of later law - Rejection of appeal request - Order for appellant to pay legal fees

The summary of the decision is as follows: - The appeal by A. X. (S.) for a retrial of the criminal case, which concluded with the issuance of the unappealable decision 809/2014 by the Tripartite Court of Thrace on March 23, 2016, was rejected as inadmissible. - The defendant A. X. (S.) was ordered to pay judicial costs amounting to 250 euros for the criminal proceedings. The decision was rendered in Athens on November 1, 2016, and issued in Athens on June 27, 2017. Key points: - The new evidence presented did not sufficiently prove that the defendant was innocent of the crime she was convicted of (receiving a bribe). - The court considered that even with the newly submitted evidence, the original conviction would likely stand due to the circumstances. - The defendant is ordered to pay 250 euros in legal fees.

Το Δίκαστοριο του Αρείου Πάγου απορρίψα την αίτηση αναιρέσεως από τον Ι.Σ. του Σ., που δεν εμφανίσθηκε στο ακροατήριο, και καταδικάστηκε στα δικαστικά έξοδα και τη δικαστική δαπάνη της πολιτικώς ενάγουσας.

The summary of the decision is as follows: - The appeal (αίτηση αναιρέσεως) against the 106/2016 decision by the Council of Investigative Judges, submitted by K. S., was rejected as inadmissible. - The person who appealed (the defendant) was fined 250 euros for this inadmissible appeal. This decision was rendered on March 8, 2017, and published in Athens on March 9, 2017. Key points: 1. The appeal against the Council of Investigative Judges' decision was deemed inadmissible. 2. The defendant (K. S.) was fined for submitting an inadmissible appeal. 3. The decision was made by the Vice President and Secretary. 4. The date of rendering and publication were March 8 and 9, 2017, respectively.

The decision annulls the judgments of the Thrimeleio (Tri-Monthly) Court of Appeals in cases 2074/2015 and 2033/2014. It refers the case back to the same court, but with a different panel of judges excluding those who previously judged it. Key points: - The Thrimeleio Court of Appeals erred by exceeding its jurisdiction in these cases. - The first instance judgments were rendered before the new legal framework took effect. - The case is sent back for reconsideration by a new panel of judges, not including those from the previous judgment. The decision was made in Athens on June 7, 2016 and published publicly on October 13, 2016. This appears to be an appellate court's ruling overturning lower court decisions due to jurisdictional issues related to when the new legal framework applied.

This decision is rejecting an appeal against a criminal decision, and includes the following key points: 1. The appeal (no. 2083 of 24-11-2015) by P.S. against a decision (no. 9256/30-9-2015) from the Third Chamber of the Thessaloniki Criminal Court is rejected. 2. The appellant (P.S.) is fined 250 euros for filing an improper appeal. 3. The decision was rendered in Athens on June 7, 2016. 4. It was published publicly at a session in the court building on September 26, 2016. The main reasons given are: - The appellant's challenge to the publication of the judgment summary in the official register is unfounded. - The appeal was filed after the time limit for appealing had expired. - The appellant's arguments do not meet the requirements set out in Article 513 of the Greek Penal Code. The decision concludes that the appeal should be rejected and the appellant fined, without accepting any of his grounds or arguments.

This case involves a dispute over jurisdiction between two Greek judicial authorities regarding an investigation into alleged fraudulent activities that took place on a Cypriot-flagged ship. The key points are: 1. The incident occurred on a ship under the Cypriot flag anchored in international waters. 2. Two suspects, N. S. and M. A., are residents of Rethymno, Crete. 3. There was a disagreement between two prosecutors from different judicial districts (Piraeus and Limassol) over which authority should handle the case. 4. The dispute was referred to the Supreme Court for resolution on jurisdiction. 5. The Supreme Court ruled that the Piraeus Prosecutor's Office and its courts have jurisdiction over this case, both in terms of the initial investigative phase and any subsequent proceedings. 6. This decision applies to a specific criminal investigation file (Α 14/129 [ΕΓ16-14/140]) against N. S. and M. A. 7. The final decision was made in Athens on May 11, 2016, and published in Athens on June 1, 2016. The case highlights the complexities of jurisdictional issues involving international waters and ships registered

This decision is a complex legal ruling, but I can summarize the key points: The court annulled (anairei) an earlier judgment from the Polytechneio First Instance Court in Athens. The reasons for this are: 1. There were financial calculation errors in the original judgment. 2. Some of the arguments used by the losing party weren't valid according to legal rules. Specifically, the court found that: - Errors in calculating certain payments - Improper application of a legal provision (Article 560 para. 1 of the Civil Procedure Code) As a result: - The original judgment is annulled - The case is sent back for further proceedings before a different panel of judges - The losing party (the one who appealed) is ordered to pay 2,300 euros in legal fees The decision was made on November 8, 2016 and published publicly at an open session on December 14, 2016.
