As a result, the court dismissed the appeal and ordered Ms. M. P. to pay 250 euros in legal fees. 5. The decision was made on November 18, 2015, and published publicly at an Athens courthouse on December 9, 2015. 6. The presiding judge of

Σύνοψη: Η απόφαση προτείνει την παραπομπή μιας εισαγγελίας (υπ’ αρ. 155031/17-2-2015) από το Πρωτοδικείο Αθηνών στο Πρωτοδικείο Πειραιά, λόγω του ότι δεν μπορεί να αποφασιστεί παραπομπή σε άλλο ομοειδές και ισόβαθμο δικαστήριο της περιφέρειάς του. Το λόγο αυτό εξηγείται με την υπαγωγή στο Εφε

This decision overturns a previous conviction and acquits the defendant. Here's a summary of the key points: 1. The case was originally decided by the Trireme Court in Athens on October 21, 2015. 2. The original conviction was for domestic bodily harm (endodromia) based on injuries to the victim's hand and fingers caused by blows from the defendant's hands. 3. The new decision finds that the original application of the relevant criminal law was incorrect due to an improper interpretation and application of key provisions. 4. Specifically, it is ruled that while there was indeed bodily harm inflicted, this did not rise to the level of "continuous behavior" required for a conviction under the more serious provision (Article 6, Paragraph 1, Subparagraph β' of Law 3500/2003). 5. Since no punishable act exists, the defendant is declared innocent (athos) of the charged offense. 6. The decision was announced publicly in Athens on November 11, 2015. 7. It overturns and annuls the previous judgment (Decision No. 392/2015). The key legal reasoning is that while there were injuries, they did not

This decision is a Greek court ruling that rejects an appeal and imposes fines on the appellant. Here's a summary: 1. The case concerns an appeal (filed 26-2-2015, no. 15/2015) against a decision by the Athens Third Instance Court. 2. The appeal was rejected for being unfounded. 3. The court ruled that: - The appellant's arguments based on alleged errors in the first-instance judgment are unacceptable. - Specifically, the court dismissed claims about the conviction of the person appealing and omissions in the judgment and minutes. 4. As a result, the appeal is rejected in full. 5. The appellant (Κ. Τ. του Γ.) was fined 250 euros for the legal costs. 6. The decision was pronounced on October 21, 2015, and published publicly at an Athens session on November 11, 2015. 7. The document is signed by the President of the Areopagus (a high court in ancient Greece) and the Secretary. The key point is that the Greek court found no valid grounds for overturning the original judgment and thus rejected the appeal while imposing a fine on the

The decision involves the case of a defendant who was found guilty by the Triamilioi Ephetai (Athens) on May 22, 2015 for distributing child pornography. The key points are: - The original verdict incorrectly applied the law regarding the element of profit motive in determining the crime. - An unauthorized review found that without this element, the act was not punishable before December 24, 2007. Key decisions: - The appeal court annulled (anairei) the original decision of the Triamilioi Ephetai. - It declared the defendant (Θ. Α.) innocent (athos). - The case will not be referred back to the lower court for reconsideration, but the correct law will be applied. The summary highlights that due to an incorrect application of the law regarding profit motive in the original verdict, and considering the date when such acts became punishable under the new law, the defendant should have been declared innocent.

The case involves a Greek prisoner challenging the decision of a three-judge panel in Athens to deny his application for early release from a life sentence for murder. The key points are: 1. The court found that under Greek and international law, particularly the European Convention on Human Rights, the 20-year minimum sentence for manslaughter could not be reduced below this term. 2. It ruled that the prisoner's case did not meet the criteria to justify an exception to this rule based on his past good behavior. 3. The court rejected the prisoner's application for early release as inadmissible and ordered him to pay 250 euros in legal fees. 4. The decision was made in Athens on May 28, 2015 and published publicly at a session of the court on June 10, 2015. 5. The reasoning included an analysis of how the UK Supreme Court had interpreted similar provisions for early release, finding that Greek law did not allow for exceptions to the minimum 20-year term even in cases of good behavior. In essence, the court upheld the mandatory 20-year minimum sentence for manslaughter and rejected the prisoner's application based on this interpretation.

This is a summary of a court decision in Greece: - The case concerns an appeal against an eviction order (αποφάσεως αναιρέσεως) from property. - The main arguments were about the validity of documents and evidence presented by the defendant. - The court rejected the appeals and upheld the original eviction decision. - It ordered the appellant to pay 2700 euros in legal fees. - The decision was made on May 19, 2015 in Athens and published publicly on May 20, 2015. The key points are that the court found no valid grounds for overturning the eviction order and ruled against the appellant.

This is a summary of a Greek court decision regarding an appeal against the confiscation of land. Here are the key points: 1. The case was initially decided by a single judge (Monomelia Protodikio) in 2013, but was overturned on appeal. 2. The main issues were: - The confiscation order did not properly apply relevant laws - There were inconsistencies in how land areas were calculated and accounted for 3. The higher court (Areopagus) ruled to: - Invalidate the original decision - Send the case back to a different single judge for further proceedings - Order the return of the confiscated property to the defendant - Impose a fine on the losing parties (defendants) of 600 Euros 4. The decision was made in Athens on May 19, 2015 and published publicly on May 20, 2015. The case appears to involve complex land ownership issues with potential disputes over inheritance or other legal claims.

Η αίτηση αναιρέσεως της υπ’ αριθμ. 4948/2014 αποφάσεως του Τριμελούς Πλημμελειοδικείου Πειραιώς, που πρότεινε ο Α.Χ. του Σ., απορρίφθηκε από το Δικαστήριο του Αρείου Πάγου λόγω έμφασης του κλήρου. Ο αναιρεσείων οδόρθηκε να εμφανιστεί, αλλά δεν παρέστη στο ακροατήριο και κρίθηκε στα δικαστικά έξοδα.

Το Δικαστήριο του ΑРЕΙΟΥ ΠΑΓΟΥ συνέδεσε την αίτηση για αναιρέσειο ενός κατηγορούμενου, που δεν κατήφθησε την προκαταβολή γραμματίου. Απορρίψε την αίτηση και κατάδικα τον κατηγορούμενο σε δικαστικά έξοδα 250 ευρώ. Δημοσιεύθηκε 21 Μαΐου 2015.

This decision is a court ruling in Greece regarding an appeal against a dismissal decision. Here's a summary of the key points: 1. The case concerns a dispute between "Demotiki Etaireia Hydreias-Apocheisias Higoumistes" (a municipal water and sewage company) and an individual named X.A. 2. The main issue is whether the appeal against a decision dismissing a claim should be accepted based on alleged violations of procedural law. 3. The court rejected the appeal, finding no valid grounds for its rejection. 4. Specifically: - The court found that the dismissal decision did not violate any fundamental principles of civil law. - It ruled that the relevant provision (Article 560 para.1 of the Civil Procedure Code) was correctly applied in this case. - The court dismissed arguments about procedural violations as unfounded. 5. As a result, the court: - Declined to annul the original dismissal decision - Condemned the appellant (X.A.) to pay 1,800 euros in legal fees 6. The decision was made on March 24, 2015, and published at an open session in Athens on May 12, 2

This case summary involves a legal dispute over the legality of an administrative decision regarding the use of a road. Here are the key points: 1. The court rejected arguments from two plaintiffs (Χ. (Χ.) Π. and Β.) against a single-instance court's decision (No. 353/2014) in Corinth. 2. The court ruled that an appeal should be admitted to the public treasury. 3. The defendant was ordered to pay judicial costs of 2,700 euros to the plaintiffs. 4. The case was judged and decided in Athens on April 30, 2015. 5. The decision was publicly announced in Athens at a public session held on May 6, 2015. 6. The presiding Areopagite (chief judge) signed the ruling. The main arguments against the appeal were found to be unfounded, and the court upheld the original administrative decision while ordering the defendant to cover legal fees for the plaintiffs.

This summary describes a court decision rejecting an appeal by the Greek State against a local authority decision. Here are the key points: 1. The case was about an appeal (aitisi) filed on October 31, 2014 by the "A.E.B.E Marble Quarries D.P. and Technical Hospitality Agricultural Transport Maritime Enterprises and Agents" company against a decision dated March 5, 2014 from the Athens Ephorate. 2. The court rejected this appeal on April 30, 2015. 3. The Greek State was ordered to pay 300 euros in legal fees to the appellant. 4. The decision was published publicly at a session held in Athens on May 6, 2015. 5. The presiding Areopagitarch (chief judge) signed and dated the decision. The summary indicates that the court upheld the original local authority decision against the Greek State's appeal, ordering it to pay costs but not overturning the underlying administrative decision.

The case involves an appeal against the decision of the Mixed Military Court of Aegean Islands regarding the discharge of an individual (referred to as "I. S.") from military service due to a conviction for assault. Key points: 1. The appellant challenged the discharge decision, arguing that he should be considered for early discharge based on his good behavior after the incident. 2. The court rejected this argument, stating there was no evidence of improper conduct by the victim leading up to the incident, nor sufficient proof of significant improvement in the appellant's behavior post-incident. 3. Specifically, the court found: - There was no indication that the victim's prior behavior had led to the assault - The fact that he remained employed as a police officer and received an honorary mention did not justify early discharge 4. As a result, the court dismissed the appeal and ordered the appellant to pay 250 euros in legal fees. 5. The decision was made on May 6, 2015, and published publicly at the Athens courthouse on May 13, 2015. In essence, the court upheld the original discharge decision, rejecting the argument for early release based on good behavior post-incident.

Η αίτηση αναίρεσης της απόφασης του Τριμελούς Εφετείου Ευβοίας (3/2014) απορρίπτεται από το Δικαστήριο του Αρείου Πάγου, καθώς οι λόγοι αναίρεσης είναι απορριπτέοι ως απαράδεκτοι. Δικαστήριο καταδίκαζε τον αναιρεσείοντα στα δικαστικά έξοδα του αναιρεσιβλήτου (2.700 ευρώ).

Here's a summary of the key points: - The Monomelic Epheta (Athens) annulled the decision of the Single Epheta (Athens) in case 4973/2013. - The case is referred back to the same Epheta, but with a different judge presiding over it. - The defendant (employer) is ordered to pay 750 euros in legal fees to the plaintiffs (employees). - This decision was made on March 24, 2015 in Athens. - It was published in an open session at the Epheta's hearing room in Athens on April 28, 2015. The main reasons for annulment were: - The Single Epheta incorrectly rejected claims regarding night work and Sunday/night shifts without requiring a specific daily or weekly hour count. - The claim for overtime pay during Sundays and evenings was considered valid based on the content of the attached tables.

This appears to be a summary of a court decision, specifically an appeal against a previous judgment. Here's a breakdown of the key points: 1. The case involves a woman (referred to as "κατηγορουμένη") who allegedly returned money to the state treasury after it had been privatized. 2. The original judgment (No. 68/2014) imposed a two-year prison sentence on her, based on when she made the return relative to the start of legal proceedings. 3. The appeal argues that this should be reconsidered in light of new laws and regulations introduced after the initial case was decided. 4. Specifically, it claims there's uncertainty about exactly when the woman returned the money, which could affect the length of her potential sentence under the new law. 5. As a result: - Part of the original judgment is set aside - The case is sent back to the same court for reconsideration by different judges - An appeal against this decision was rejected 6. The final decision was made in Athens on April 22, 2015 and published publicly on May 5, 2015. 7. It's signed by the Deputy President

This decision is regarding an appeal against a judgment by two individuals, Ά. Σ. του Β. και Α. Μ. του Π., who are accused of wrongful dismissal (anairésis). The key points are: 1. The court rejected their joint appeal for discharge based on lack of hearing, lack of specific and experienced causation, and incorrect interpretation and application of the law. 2. The court found that the two individuals were responsible for wrongful dismissal in relation to a judgment dated 228/2014 by the Trikala Regional Court. 3. They were sentenced to pay judicial costs of 250 euros each and a fine of 500 euros to the state. 4. The decision was made in Athens on April 22, 2015 and published publicly at an open session in the court on April 29, 2015. 5. The presiding judge was the Deputy President and Secretary General (ΑΝΤΙΠΡΟΕΔΡΟΣ Ο ΓΡΑΜΜΑΤΕΑΣ). The decision essentially upheld the original judgment against these two individuals for wrongful dismissal and imposed financial penalties on them.

The summary of the decision is as follows: - The appeal against the 161/2014 decision by Mr. R.-B. G. from May 8, 2014 was rejected. - The person appealing (referred to as "the accused") was fined 250 euros for the appeal. - This decision was made in Athens on April 1, 2015 and published publicly at an open session of the court on April 23, 2015. The reasons given for rejecting the appeal include: - The lack of specific and substantiated grounds for challenging the original decision - Errors in interpreting and applying criminal provisions - Absence of a legal basis The court found all the arguments presented by the appellant to be unfounded.

Here's a summary of the key points: - The appeal against the conviction for issuing an unregistered warrant was dismissed. - The defendant(s) (O. P. - K., N.) failed to provide a valid reason for overturning the decision. - The court upheld the original conviction and sentence of 250 euros in legal fees. - The case was heard in Athens on April 1, 2015. - The judgment was publicly announced at the courthouse in Athens on April 7, 2015. The main reasons for dismissing the appeal were: 1. The alleged offense (unregistered warrant) occurred more than 8 years before the second trial and thus did not qualify for paragraph 3 of Article 370. 2. There was no other valid reason to overturn the decision. The court rejected the appeal with costs, meaning the defendant(s) must pay the legal fees.

The summary of the case is as follows: - The defendant, X. X., appealed against a decision (632/2014) by the Trimerial Council of Thrace. - He requested an appeal on the grounds that he was unable to appear in court due to his work in Germany and because he was being prosecuted for a similar case at a later date. - The court rejected this appeal, stating: - The only evidence presented (a witness statement by M. X.) did not justify the request for postponement based on higher urgency. - His employment in Germany does not constitute higher urgency as it prevents him from appearing in court. - There was no document proving he would be prosecuted for a similar case at a later date, which would have justified postponing the hearing. - The court rejected his appeal and fined him 250 euros for abuse of process. - The decision was made on April 1, 2015, in Athens. - It was publicly announced in Athens on April 2, 2015.

Here's a summary of the key points: - The appeal to annul Decision No. 231/2014 by defendant T. was rejected. - Defendant T. was found guilty and ordered to pay: - 250 Euros in legal fees - 500 Euros in state expenses - The decision was rendered on March 18, 2015 in Athens. - It was publicly announced at the courthouse in Athens on March 24, 2015. The main arguments that led to rejecting the appeal were: - The court had already formed a judicial opinion based on other evidence - Photos of the accident site adequately identified themselves without further details needed - The defendant did not object to their review In essence, the higher court upheld the lower court's decision and rejected the defendant's attempt to overturn it.

The summary of the decision is as follows: - The appeal by N.S. (A.) against the 103α, 103, 104, 105, 107/2013, 31/2014 decisions of the Mixed Military Court of Appeal in Nauplio was rejected. - The defendant (N.S. A.) was condemned to pay judicial expenses amounting to 250 euros and political supporters D.T., I.T.-T., P.T. were ordered to pay 500 euros in judicial costs. - The relevant articles from the Penal Code (articles 26 para. 1, 27 para. 1, 51, 52, 83, 84 para. 2 point e and 299 para. 1) were appended to decision 103α, 103, 104, 105, 107/2013, 31/2014 of the Mixed Military Court of Appeal in Nauplio. - The decision was rendered on March 4, 2015 in Athens and published

This decision involves a case where an individual (Β. Σ.) appealed against a fine imposed by the Triamilioi Ephetai of Evia, Greece. The key points are: 1. The appeal was filed on November 24, 2014. 2. The original decision (No. 353/2014) was upheld in part and rejected in part: - The main arguments presented by the appellant were found to be without merit. - Specifically, it was ruled that there was no violation of Article 171(1) of the Greek Penal Code regarding the right to a hearing for the accused. 3. The decision states that the appeal is inadmissible and should be dismissed as baseless (ουσιαστικά αβάσιμη). 4. As a result, the appellant was ordered to pay judicial expenses amounting to 250 Euros. 5. The case was heard in Athens on March 4, 2015, and the decision was made public during an open session at the court on March 18, 2015. 6. The presiding judge is referred to as "ΑΝΤΙ

This is a Greek legal decision rejecting an appeal and imposing fines. Here's a summary of the key points: 1. The case concerns an appeal against a 2014 decision by the Athens Court of Appeals. 2. The appellant (Η. Π. Δ.) challenged a fine of 10 euros per day for one year of imprisonment, arguing it was excessive and improperly applied. 3. The court rejected this argument: - It found that the original court properly applied relevant criminal law provisions on conversion of penalties. - The appeal's main ground based on improper interpretation or application of essential criminal law provisions was unfounded. 4. As a result, the court dismissed the appeal in full. 5. The appellant and his representative company were fined: - 250 euros for legal costs - 500 euros for the cost of politically motivated representation 6. The decision was made on February 24, 2015, published in Athens on March 4, 2015. 7. The presiding judge was the Deputy President and Secretary. The decision essentially upheld the original fine while imposing additional costs on the appellant for bringing what it considered an unfounded appeal.

The summary of the case is as follows: - The defendant (A. O.) appealed a decision by the Athens Ephetae Council to reject his appeal against a 1921/2014 indictment. - The court rejected this appeal, finding it inadmissible. - The court upheld the rejection of the indictment and fined the appellant 250 euros. Key points: - The case was heard in Athens on February 24, 2015 - The decision was made by the Deputy Procurator - The court found that the defendant's appeal did not meet the criteria for admissibility under Article 6(1) of the European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR) - The court rejected the appeal and imposed a fine of 250 euros The summary provides the main outcome - rejection of the appeal and imposition of a fine - while also giving context about the legal basis and timing of the decision.

This is a Greek court decision regarding an appeal against a previous judicial decision. Here's a summary of the key points: - The case concerns an appeal filed by E.P.S. (a pseudonym) on October 24, 2014, challenging a decision from the Athens Ephetae Triad (5531/2014). - The court rejected this appeal. - The defendant (the person who appealed) was found guilty and fined 250 euros for legal costs. - This decision was rendered in Athens on February 18, 2015, and announced publicly at the courthouse on February 24, 2015. - The court rejected the appeal based on several grounds: - There was no valid reason to overturn the previous decision. - The defendant's claim for leniency (under Article 84 para. 2 of the Penal Code) was not substantiated with specific facts. - The reasons given in the previous decision were sufficient. - The court explicitly states that it finds the appeal unfounded and rejects it, while also imposing a fine on the defendant for legal costs. The overall conclusion is that the lower court's decision stands, and the defendant must pay

This decision is from the Supreme Court of Greece, specifically the Trihellenic (Plommematwn) of Eastern Crete. Here's a summary: 1. The case concerns an appeal against a conviction for tax evasion. 2. The convicted individual was found guilty of falsely reporting VAT returns and failing to pay taxes due on services provided by his company, Eurodomi. 3. The court upheld the original decision, rejecting the defendant's appeal. 4. Key points: - The court found that the lower court had provided a detailed and specific rationale for its decision. - It rejected claims of insufficient or inadequate reasoning. - The court found no grounds to overturn the conviction based on procedural issues. 5. As a result, the original sentence was upheld: - The defendant was ordered to pay 250 euros in legal fees. - He was also convicted and sentenced (though the exact sentence is not specified). 6. The decision was made public at an open session of the court on February 24, 2015. 7. The case involved complex financial documents and testimonies from witnesses. The summary indicates a clear rejection of the defendant's appeal by the highest Greek judicial body for this region.

This summary describes a court decision rejecting an appeal and sentencing the appellant to legal fees. Here are the key points: - The case was about rejecting an appeal against a previous ruling. - The appeal was filed by N.T. (D) on October 13, 2014 regarding the 4513/2013 decision of the Athens Tripartite Court. - The court rejected the appeal for two main reasons: 1. Lack of specific and binding reasoning in rejecting a previous request 2. Exceeding jurisdiction by ruling on matters within the competence of political courts - As a result, the appellant (D) was sentenced to: - 250 euros in legal fees - 500 euros for the legal costs of two political representatives (S.D. and S.H.) - The decision was rendered in Athens on February 11, 2015 and published publicly at an open session in the court on February 18, 2015. The summary highlights the court's rejection of the appeal based on procedural grounds and the imposition of legal costs as a penalty.

The case involved a challenge to the validity of a will, specifically regarding the legal opinion that declared it invalid. Here's a summary: - The original decision (941/86) by the Epheta (highest court in Greece for civil and criminal cases) ruled the will invalid due to alleged forgery. - A new appeal was filed against this decision, arguing that the Epheta's reasoning was inadequate. Key points of the appeal: - The Epheta did not adequately explain how it contradicted its own previous findings about the authenticity of signatures on documents supporting the will. - The court found the Epheta's reasoning insufficient and thus invalid. As a result: 1. The original decision (265/2013) by the Epheta of Larissa was annulled. 2. The case was referred back to the same Epheta, but with different judges. 3. The applicant was ordered to return the submitted appeal fee. 4. The applicant was fined 2,000 euros for an unjustified legal expense. The final decision was made in Athens on December 16, 2014, and published publicly at a session of the court in Athens on February 4, 2015. The deputy president

Here's a summary of the key points: - The court rejected an appeal and additional grounds from K. K. against an order by the Athens Epheta regarding a property case. - The court ruled that there was no valid reason for the appeal, as all relevant evidence had been considered in the original decision. - It found fault with the Epheta's failure to consider certain testimony from witness I.-G. Δ., which supported K. K.'s claim of poor economic circumstances. - As a result, it rejected the appeal and ordered the case to be entered in the public treasury. - The losing party (K. K.) was fined 2700 Euros for legal costs. The decision was made on December 3, 2014 in Athens and published publicly on January 14, 2015.

This is a Greek court decision rejecting an appeal to overturn a previous judgment. Here's a summary: - The case involves the "Anaireseion" (an entity) appealing against a 2013 judgment from the Single Judge of Athens. - The appellant claims they did not exercise suspension rights, but there's no evidence of this in the documents provided. - The court finds that the appeal was filed without exercising any suspension rights and is therefore invalid. - As a result, the court rejects the appeal. - The appellant (Anaireseion) is ordered to pay 300 euros in legal fees to each of the respondents. Key points: 1. Rejection of the appeal 2. No evidence of non-exercise of suspension rights 3. Appellant ordered to pay legal costs The decision was made on December 9, 2014 and published on December 15, 2014 in an open session at the court in Athens.

The summary of the decision is as follows: - The appeal by E. K. and others against the 83/2013 decision of the Dodecanese Court / Transitional Head Office was rejected. - The case will be entered into the public treasury of the complainant. - The appellants (E. K. and others) were sentenced to bear the costs of the defendant's legal proceedings, amounting to 2,700 euros. The decision was made in Athens on November 18, 2014, and published publicly at a session held in the court building in Athens on December 3, 2014.

The summary of the decision is as follows: - The appeal to revoke the 586/2013 decision by the Single Judge of Piraeus was rejected. - The appellant (referred to as "the litigant") was condemned to pay judicial expenses amounting to 1,100 euros. - The appellant was ordered to return a fine of 300 euros. This decision was rendered in Athens on November 4, 2014 and published publicly at the same location on November 18, 2014.

This decision addresses an appeal against a previous judgment regarding a dispute over a contract for services. The key points are: - The original case (2797/2012) was partially overturned. - The part of the case related to the service contract is being sent back to the same court, but with different judges. - The party that lost in part (the "anaireseisvita") is ordered to pay 1,000 euros in legal fees to the winning party ("anaireseiousa"). - This decision was made on October 29, 2014, and published on November 10, 2014. The main reasons for this partial overturning were: 1. The second court (appeals court) failed to properly examine the primary basis of the case related to the service contract when it was first heard. 2. This oversight violated Article 559(8) of the Civil Procedure Code, which requires a re-examination of rejected secondary bases by the appeals court if an appeal is filed. The decision specifically states that only part of the original judgment has been overturned and refers to the case number for further proceedings in the same court with new judges.

This is a Greek legal judgment rejecting an appeal against a decision by the Thessaloniki Ephorie (a local administrative body). The key points are: 1. The appeal was filed on September 25, 2009 to overturn a decision from the Thessaloniki Ephorie dated June 2647/2006. 2. The court rejected the appeal for several reasons: - The Ephorie's decision had a clear and sufficient legal basis - It properly applied relevant laws (Articles 348, 1096, 1098 of the Code of Organization and Operation of Courts) - There were no grounds to question its factual findings 3. As a result: - The appeal was dismissed - The appellant (E.A.) was ordered to pay the costs of both parties - The amount was set at 2,700 euros for each party 4. The judgment was publicly announced in Athens on July 1, 2014. 5. It was signed by the Deputy President and Secretary General. The case appears to involve a dispute over property or resources related to university buildings, with the Ephorie's decision being upheld against an appeal.

This decision addresses an appeal against a previous ruling by the Athens Ephetae (a Greek court) regarding a claim for the enforcement of a property transfer agreement. The key points are: 1. The appeal was based on alleged procedural irregularities and lack of legal basis in the original judgment. 2. The higher court found that: - The 5/4/1994 receipt, which purported to agree to sell the property for 1,000,000 drachmas, was genuine at the time. - However, no formal agreement or transfer of the property had actually been made. 3. As a result: - The original judgment was annulled - The case was remitted back to the same court for further proceedings with a new panel of judges - The appellant (who initiated the appeal) was ordered to pay 3,000 euros in legal costs 4. The decision was made public during an open session at the court building in Athens on August 29, 2014. The summary indicates that there were procedural issues with the original ruling and that a formal property transfer agreement had not been properly established despite what was claimed in documents presented.

Here's a summary of the key points: - The appeal against the decision by the Athens Epheta (high court) dated 30 November 2007, numbered 3454/2007, was rejected. - The defendant (appeal applicant) was ordered to pay 300 euros in legal fees for the non-litigant parties. - This decision was made in Athens on June 12, 2014 and published publicly at a session of the court in Athens on June 26, 2014. - The document includes signatures of the Deputy President and Secretary. The main points are that the appeal failed and there will be a fee for legal representation of the non-litigant parties.

Here's a summary of the key points: - The case concerns an appeal for annulment of a decision from the Athens Ephorie (local court) dated 1388/2009, filed by three individuals on June 25, 2009. - The original hearing was adjourned due to parliamentary elections in November 2009. - A new hearing was set for September 18, 2013, but the defendants did not appear. - On April 2, 2014, the final hearing took place without the defendants present or a substitute lawyer. No one from the defendants appeared in court. - An unauthorized investigation found that the defendants had not been legally summoned to attend the hearing. - The decision declares the hearing of June 25, 2009 invalid and annuls it as unacceptable. - This decision was made on May 27, 2014 and published on June 24, 2014 in an open session at the court building in Athens.

The case involves an appeal against a decision by the Ephoreia of Athens (a local administrative body) regarding the invalidation of a legal argument. The key points are: 1. The appellant (A.K.) challenged a decision from September 29, 2011 and additional arguments from April 2, 2013. 2. The court rejected these appeals for invalidity. 3. The court found that the Ephoreia of Athens had not properly examined part one of the appeal's argument regarding an incorrect application of law by considering a challenged term "D" as void due to being a forbidden inheritance contract under Article 368 of the Civil Code. 4. However, the court ruled that this argument was actually considered and rejected by the Ephoreia in substance. 5. As a result, all grounds were dismissed. 6. The appellant (A.K.) was ordered to pay judicial costs amounting to 2700 Euros. 7. The decision was made on May 27, 2014 in Athens and published in an open session at the court building on June 26, 2014. 8. A Vice President of the court (Vice Chairman) signed the judgment. The

Here's a summary of the decision: The court rejected an appeal by P. G. and Z. S. against a 2013 decision by the Athens Epheta (high court) regarding inheritance case 2784/2013. Key points: - The appeal was dismissed for multiple reasons, including issues with the reasoning of the original decision - The case will be recorded in the public treasury register - The appellants (P. G. and Z. S.) were ordered to pay a fine of 2700 euros for their unlawful appeal The decision was made on April 10, 2014 and published on June 18, 2014 in Athens. The undersigned is the Deputy Secretary (ΑΝΤΙΠΡΟΕΔΡΟΣ Η ΓΡΑΜΜΑΤΕΑΣ) who signed the decision.

Here's a summary of the key points from the decision: - The court rejected an appeal by Δ. Τ. to overturn a 2012 decision by the Aegean Regional Court (Metabatiki Edra Samou) regarding the right of residence for the appellant and his family. - The court found that the grounds presented in the appeal were not valid, as they did not provide sufficient or contradictory reasons for the acquisition of rights through inheritance rather than through the primary method. - Specifically, it was determined that: - The appellant failed to prove he acquired rights over the property through use. - There was a violation of Article 1041 of the Greek Civil Code regarding the conditions for acquiring rights by use. - The reasons given for acquisition via inheritance were insufficient and contradictory. - As a result, the court: - Declined to overturn the previous decision - Ordered the case to be entered in the public treasury (Article 495 para. 4 of the Greek Civil Procedure Code) - Fined the appellant €1,800 for legal costs - The decision was signed by the Vice-President and Secretary on May 6, 2014 - It was published at

This case involves a dispute over the ownership of immovable property in Heraklion, Greece. The key points are: 1. The Metropolitan Archbishop Syrou D.A.S. claimed title to the property based on an earlier decision from 1979. 2. The Municipality of Heraklion contested this claim, arguing it had acquired the property through a legal process. 3. The case was heard by the Supreme Administrative Court (Areios Pagos) and initially rejected the Municipality's appeal. 4. Two judges dissented, particularly Judge Ioannis Sideris, arguing that: - There was an earlier unilateral declaration of ownership by the Metropolitan in 1979 - This constitutes a prior claim to the property - The current case should be referred back to the full Areios Pagos for reconsideration 5. Due to the narrow margin (one vote) in favor of the dissenting opinion, the case was sent to the full Areios Pagos for final decision. 6. The Supreme Administrative Court ultimately ruled against the Municipality and upheld the Metropolitan's claim on June 3, 2014. The key legal issues revolve around the priority of claims to property ownership and whether earlier unilateral declarations can be considered

Here's a summary of the key points in Greek: The court rejected an appeal against a decision by a single judge not to allow a lawyer's discharge application. The main reasons were: 1. The court found that only some, but not all, elements supporting the claim of improper exercise of rights had been proven. 2. The lawyer submitted proposals without a valid and substantiated request from the client. 3. The court ruled that the discharged lawyer must pay 1800 euros in legal fees to the other party. 4. The decision was made on June 10, 2014 in Athens and published on June 17, 2014. 5. The appeal was rejected for lack of sufficient grounds. The summary captures the essence of the court's decision, the reasons given, and the outcome without reproducing any copyrighted text.

This is a Greek court decision, specifically an appeal judgment. Here's a summary of the key points: 1. The Court overturned a previous ruling by the Athens Bench and sent the case back to that same bench for further examination. 2. The main reasons given were: - The original ruling was considered inadequate in its treatment of certain aspects - There was an error in how the article 559, para 14 of the Civil Procedure Code was applied 3. The plaintiffs (the losing party) were ordered to pay 2,300 euros in legal fees to the defendant. 4. The case was dismissed and sent back for further proceedings on June 10, 2014 in Athens. 5. The decision was signed by the Deputy President and Secretary of the court. The key issue appears to be a procedural error or misapplication of civil procedure law that led the higher court to overturn the lower court's ruling and send it back for reconsideration.

This decision is a court ruling in Greece regarding an appeal against the denial of inheritance rights. Here's a summary: 1. The case concerns two siblings (αναιρεσιβλήτοι) challenging their older brother's (αναιρεσείων) claim to part of their late parents' estate. 2. The court ruled that the younger siblings did not have valid grounds to contest the earlier decision by the Epheta (a Greek judicial body). 3. Key points: - The court found no issues with how the Epheta applied legal principles regarding inheritance and property rights. - It rejected arguments from the older brother about his right to inherit certain assets. - The court upheld the Epheta's decision. 4. As a result, the court: - Rejected the appeal filed on December 21, 2011 - Ordered the older brother to pay 2700 euros in legal fees 5. The ruling was made in Athens on April 30, 2014 and published in a public session at the court on June 3, 2014. 6. The decision is based on Greek Civil Procedure Code articles 103

This decision is a court ruling that rejects an appeal for the annulment of a judgment. Here's a summary: - The case concerns an inheritance dispute, specifically regarding property left in a will. - An individual (Α. Κ.) appealed a decision by a single judge in Western Macedonia to reject his claim about the scope of property bequeathed in the will. - The higher court (in Athens) rejected this appeal on two grounds: 1. The lower court's interpretation and application of the will was correct, based on the evidence presented. 2. The appellant (αναιρεσείοντας) should be held responsible for legal fees related to his unsuccessful appeal. - As a result, the court: - Declined to annul the original judgment - Ordered the appellant's case to be entered in the public treasury as a debt - Imposed a fine of 2700 euros on the appellant The decision was made on April 30, 2014 and published in an Athens court session on May 27, 2014. This summary captures the key points of the judicial ruling without reproducing any copyrighted material.

This is a Greek legal judgment summary. Here's a summary of the key points: - The case concerns an appeal against a decision by the Ioannina Ephoreia (a local administrative body) regarding a will. - The main arguments were: - That there was no limited property rights as claimed in the will - That inheritance of property rights should be recognized instead - The court rejected these arguments and upheld the original decision - It ruled that the Ioannina Ephoreia correctly applied relevant legal provisions - The court ordered the appellant to pay 1,800 euros in legal fees - The judgment was made public on May 7, 2014 in Athens The case appears to involve a dispute over property rights and inheritance between family members. The court ultimately supported the original decision of the Ephoreia regarding will interpretation and property rights.

This case involves a legal dispute over inheritance rights. The key points are: - A second illegitimate daughter (referred to as "έδετα" or "έδητα") challenged an earlier court decision in favor of her older sister, arguing she should inherit 1/8 of the estate instead of 1/8. - The Epheta (appeals court) upheld the original decision and ruled that the second daughter's share is 7/24 of the estate, after deducting what was already given to the first daughter. - The Epheta rejected arguments that the earlier decision should be overturned based on the general interpretation rules in Article 173 of the Civil Procedure Code. - The Epheta dismissed an appeal from the second daughter's lawyer dated September 20, 2011 against the October 2011 decision. - The Epheta ordered the two illegitimate daughters to pay 2,700 euros in legal fees for their separate representation. The case was heard and decided in Athens on April 1, 2014. The judgment was publicly announced at the court in Athens on May 7, 2014. In summary, the Epheta upheld the original

The case involves an appeal against a decision by the Athens Ephorie (a local administrative body) regarding the guardianship of an incapacitated individual, referred to as the "anaireseionta" or defendant. The key points are: 1. An appeal was filed on November 22, 2012, challenging Decision No. 4105/2011 by the Athens Ephorie. 2. The court found that there were errors in the assessment of evidence regarding the capacity to enter into legal agreements for two of the documents (the first two out of three). 3. Specifically: - It was incorrectly assumed that the incapacitated individual had the capacity to enter into legal agreements, based on a misinterpretation of the relevant documents. - The court also did not properly base its decision on the content of these documents. 4. As a result, the fifth ground for appeal (regarding these two documents) is rejected as inadmissible. 5. The court rejects the appeal entirely and orders the case to be entered into the public treasury due to the filing of a complaint. 6. The defendant (anaireseionta) is ordered to pay 2700 euros in legal costs. 7. The decision was

This summary describes a court decision rejecting an appeal for annulment of a previous judgment and imposing legal costs on the appellant. Here are the key points: 1. The case involves four first-degree heirs (αναιρεσιβλήτοι) challenging a judgment from the Athens Epheta. 2. The appeal was filed by A. G. Vasilakis on November 14, 2011 against decision no. 4371/2011 of the Epheta of Athens. 3. The court rejected the appeal and upheld the original judgment. 4. The appellant (αναιρεσιούχος) was ordered to pay legal costs amounting to 2,700 euros for the four first-degree heirs. 5. The decision was rendered in Athens on March 18, 2014 and published at a public session held in the court building in Athens on May 7, 2014. 6. The case appears to involve inheritance or property rights disputes between family members. The summary indicates that the court found no valid grounds for overturning the original judgment and imposed financial penalties on the appellant as a result of losing the appeal.

This decision is about an appeal against a previous decision by the Ephorate of Aegae (Chios) regarding a case involving I. M.-Z., who was already involved in legal proceedings. Key points: 1. The Ephorate of Aegae had previously issued Decision No. 10/2013, which is being appealed by I.M.-Z. 2. The appeal argues that the decision should be reconsidered because: - It led to an unjust delay in the case - It caused unnecessary expenses for the applicant 3. The court finds these arguments valid and rejects the appeal. 4. As a result, the case is referred back for full examination (reconsideration). 5. The plaintiff's lawyer is ordered to pay 2700 euros in legal fees for filing an unfounded appeal. 6. This decision was made on April 10, 2014, and published at a public session held on April 30, 2014, in Athens. The summary indicates that the court upheld its previous decision and rejected the appeal, while also penalizing the plaintiff's lawyer for filing an unfounded appeal.

This decision is about an appeal against a decision by the Epheta (a Greek administrative court) to deny an application for reimbursement of expenses. Here are the key points: 1. The case concerns an application from N. P. for reimbursement of expenses related to legal proceedings, specifically regarding a decision by the Epheta Thessaloniki. 2. The Epheta Thessaloniki had previously ruled against N. P.'s request for reimbursement. 3. In this appeal, it was argued that there were significant errors in the original decision due to misinterpretation of relevant laws and regulations. 4. The appeal court (Epheta Athens) found that the original decision did indeed contain errors related to the interpretation of Article 4, Paragraphs 1 and 2 of Law 3127/2003. 5. As a result, the Epheta Athens: - Rejected N. P.'s application for reimbursement - Ordered the case to be entered in the public treasury (for the appellant) - Imposed judicial costs on N. P., amounting to 1800 euros for the first instance and 300 euros for the second instance 6. The decision was made on April 1,

This document appears to be a legal judgment or decision regarding an appeal against a decision by the Aegean Ephorate (a local administrative body). Here's a summary of the key points: 1. The case involves the island of Serifos and the Cyclades Prefecture appealing a decision made by the Aegean Ephorate on December 21, 2011. 2. The appeal was submitted on December 14, 2011, but lacked proper authorization from the Economic Committee, which is required for such appeals according to relevant laws. 3. The Economic Committee's decision (dated October 16, 2012) approving the appeal was deemed invalid because it did not include a prior legal opinion as mandated by law. 4. Since the representative of the prefecture, who is the deputy governor, did not have valid authorization to submit the appeal, the court ruled that the hearing on this case should be considered unacceptable (inadmissible). 5. As a result, the court declared the appeal process invalid and rejected the prefecture's attempt to overturn the original decision. 6. The judgment was made in Athens on March 18, 2014, and published on April 10, 20

The summary of the decision is as follows: - The appeal by I. K. and E.-S. K. against the 23 December 2011 decision of the Athens Ephorie was rejected. - The appellants (I. K. and E.-S. K.) were condemned to pay judicial costs amounting to 1,800 Euros. - This decision was made in Athens on March 18, 2014. - It was published publicly during a session at the Ephorie of Athens on April 10, 2014. The reasons for this decision are detailed as follows: - The Ephorie did not violate Article 139 of the Civil Code by interpreting and applying it correctly based on the facts presented. - There was "knowledge of the fictitious nature" of the earlier child support during its issuance. - The arguments against the decision were found inadequate, particularly regarding the second ground which relied on the knowledge or potential knowledge of the fictitious nature. - The appellants as unsuccessful plaintiffs should be held liable for judicial costs.

This appears to be a Greek court decision rejecting an appeal against an earlier judgment. Here's a summary of the key points: - The case concerns an appeal by someone (referred to as "the appellant" or "anαιρεσείουσα") against a decision of the Athens Court of Appeals from May 30, 2013. - The main arguments rejected were: - That there was a violation of Article 559 paragraphs 8 and 16 of the Code of Civil Procedure - That the court violated certain statutory provisions by applying the wrong wage scale for calculating benefits - The court found these arguments to be unfounded (αβάσιμοι) - It rejected the appeal and ordered the appellant to pay 1,800 euros in legal fees to the other party - The decision was made on March 20, 2014 and published in an open session of the court on April 10, 2014 - The document is signed by the Deputy President and Secretary The case seems to involve a dispute over wage benefits or compensation, likely related to employment law. The court upheld the earlier decision against the appellant's arguments.

Here's a summary of the key points: - The case was reviewed by the Supreme Administrative Court (Epheta) in Athens. - The court overturned the decision of the lower Epheta court that had rejected the claim. - It referred the case back to the same Epheta court, which could now include other judges not involved before. - The court ordered the losing party to pay 2,700 euros in legal fees to the winning party. - The decision was made on April 8, 2014 and published at a public session on April 10, 2014 in Athens. The summary captures the main outcome (overturning the lower court's decision), key parties involved, amount awarded for legal fees, and date of the final decision.

This case involves an employee who was dismissed and sought to challenge the decision. The key points are: - An employee challenged a dismissal decision by his employer. - The initial appeal to the Epheta (Athens Court of Appeal) was rejected in decision 198/2013. - On further review, it was found that the Epheta had incorrectly interpreted certain legal provisions and failed to consider key arguments presented by the employee. - Specifically, the Epheta erred by not recognizing that the employer's new employer (successor) had entered into an independent employment contract with the employee after his termination from the original employer. - As a result, the higher court (Epheta) decision was annulled and the case remanded for further proceedings before a different panel of judges. The key legal issues were: 1. Whether the new employer could claim to be a successor to the original employment relationship 2. The proper interpretation of relevant labor law provisions regarding employee rights in such situations The final ruling was that the initial Epheta decision was annulled, the case remanded for further proceedings before a different panel of judges, and the dismissed employee was ordered to pay 1500 euros in legal costs. This summary captures the main points of the

Here's a summary of the key points: - The case concerns an appeal against an earlier decision by the Ephoriate of Athens regarding inheritance. - The main arguments for appeal were rejected: - The court found that the claims made by the person seeking to overturn the decision (the "anairesioussa") were not valid as they did not constitute relevant facts under the specified meaning. - The reasons given for the appeal, including one based on a supposed misinterpretation of her intentions, were deemed unacceptable. - As a result: - The appeal from May 3, 2009 regarding Decision No. 6787/2008 was rejected. - The person who sought to overturn the decision (the "anairesioussa") was ordered to pay court costs of 2,700 euros to those seeking to uphold it. - The judgment was pronounced in Athens on March 4, 2014 and published publicly at an open session in the courthouse on April 2, 2014. The summary focuses on the key legal findings and outcomes without reproducing any copyrighted text.

Here's a summary of the key points: - The appeal court overturned the decision of the Larissa Court of First Instance (264/2009) that had rejected the claim. - It referred the case back to the same court, but with a different panel of judges. - The court ruled against the former landowners and ordered them to pay 300 euros in legal fees to the Greek State. - This decision was made on February 18, 2014 in Athens and published in an open session at the courthouse on March 19, 2014. The court found that: - The Larissa Court's decision lacked sufficient evidence regarding key aspects of the case - It failed to properly examine whether the former landowners had correctly applied certain legal provisions for long-term use rights - There was insufficient information about which parts of the land were cultivated vs. grazed In essence, the court deemed the original ruling inadequate and sent it back for a more thorough review by other judges.

Here's a summary of the key points: - The case concerns an appeal against a decision by the Piraeus Epheta (a Greek court) in 2010. - Two grounds for annulment were rejected: 1. That the Epheta considered facts not proposed or failed to consider relevant facts 2. An argument about the father's capacity to make a will was deemed invalid - The appeal was dismissed on February 18, 2014 in Athens. - The defendant (L. N.) was ordered to pay 2700 euros in legal costs. - The decision was publicly announced in Athens on March 19, 2014. The case appears to involve a dispute over inheritance or guardianship, with the Epheta's decision being challenged by L.N., presumably one of the parties involved.

This summary covers the key points of a court decision regarding an appeal against an earlier judgment: - The case was about rejecting an appeal filed by the party seeking to overturn a previous ruling. - The court rejected the appeal on February 28, 2013, maintaining the original judgment (160/2012) from Lamia District Court. - The appealing party (referred to as "the defendant" or "anairesiousa") was found guilty and ordered to pay 1,800 euros in legal fees. - The decision was rendered in Athens on March 4, 2014. - It was publicly announced at the court in Athens on March 18, 2014. The summary highlights that the appeal was unsuccessful and the original ruling stood.

The case involves an appeal against a decision by the Polytechneio (High Court) of Volos regarding additional compensation for employees working full-time in hospital staff positions. The key points are: 1. The employee appealed a 2010 decision from the Volos High Court, arguing they should receive additional daily compensation as per Article 2, paragraph 3 of Law 201/1975. 2. The Polytechneio (High Court) of Athens upheld this appeal, ruling that: - The employees were entitled to the additional 2-hour daily compensation - They had been working full-time positions in hospital staff roles when the law was implemented - The relevant legal framework still applied during their employment period 3. The Volos High Court's original decision from 2010 was overturned. 4. The appeal against this ruling by the Athens Polytechneio (High Court) was rejected on March 4, 2014. 5. The decision was publicly announced in Athens on March 18, 2014. In essence, the higher court upheld the lower court's decision that full-time hospital staff were entitled to additional daily compensation as per the relevant law.

This appears to be a summary of a Greek court decision regarding an employment dispute. Here's a breakdown of the key points: 1. The court rejected an appeal (from July 5, 2011) seeking to overturn a 2008 decision by the Polytechneio First Instance Court. 2. It found that only one of the five grounds for appeal was valid - related to the first invalid ground mentioned. 3. The court declared void the 2008 decision regarding the first invalid ground (involving E. V.). 4. It upheld the case and accepted an appeal request from the employer. 5. The court annulled a final decision by the Peace Court of Giannitissas dated October 10, 2007. 6. It rejected the appeal in relation to this first invalid ground. 7. The employer was found liable for legal fees of 300 euros. 8. The decision was rendered in Athens on March 4, 2014 and published publicly at the court on March 18, 2014. 9. The Vice President of the Registry signed the decision. The case seems to be about invalid employment contracts or agreements that were not recognized as valid

This decision concerns the admissibility of a hearing for an appeal against a 2007 decision by the Dodecanese Ephorate. The key points are: 1. The case was initially referred to the Areios Pagos (Supreme Court) on October 19, 2011. 2. It was adjourned until December 5, 2012 for a hearing. 3. On January 22, 2013, the case was re-listed for hearing regarding the appeal. 4. The summonses to the appellants were issued on September 20, 2013 by U.S. authorities (International Judicial Assistance Office). 5. However, due to missing documents, the delivery of these summonses was not carried out. 6. Based on this, the Supreme Court ruled that the hearing is inadmissible. 7. The decision was rendered in Athens on January 22, 2014 and published in a public session at the court on March 5, 2014. 8. The Vice President (Procurator) signed the decision. The main reason for declaring the hearing inadmissible appears to be the failure to properly deliver summonses

This appears to be a Greek court decision rejecting an appeal against an earlier ruling by a municipal court. The key points are: - The appeal was rejected on 24 October 2012 for case number 1789/2011. - The case concerns the Municipality of Thessaloniki (Thessaloniki being the second largest city in Greece). - The decision orders: - The introduction of the complaint into the public treasury - A fine of 2,700 euros against those who appealed - The ruling was given in Athens on February 4, 2014 and published publicly on March 5, 2014. - It appears to be a case involving some kind of dispute over property or municipal assets between parties (referred to as the "anairesitontes" - those who appealed). The decision rejects arguments that were made in the appeal, finding them unconvincing. The court finds sufficient grounds for recognizing an infringement by the appealing parties and imposes a fine on them. Without more context about the specific case details, this summary captures the main legal points of the decision as presented.

This decision is about a case that has been referred to the Full Committee of Areios Pagos (Greek Supreme Court) for its opinion on a matter of exceptional importance. The key points are: 1. A lower court ruled in favor of an employee, awarding him 585 euros based on a difference between what he should have received and what he actually got. 2. The employer (an organization) appealed this decision, arguing that the lower court misinterpreted constitutional provisions related to equal treatment. 3. However, due to the referral of this case to the Full Committee for its opinion on a matter of exceptional importance, the current case is being suspended until the Full Committee issues its final ruling. 4. The reason for this suspension is that the issue at hand affects a large category of workers and has been referred as an issue of exceptional importance. 5. The decision states that the progress of the current case should be halted until the Full Committee's final opinion is given, to avoid issuing contradictory decisions. 6. The case was officially published in a public session on February 14, 2014. In essence, this is a procedural decision to wait for a higher court's opinion before proceeding with the lower court's ruling.

This decision overturns a previous ruling by the Athens Ephetae (a Greek court) in case 5948/2011, specifically regarding the part that referred to the disputed issue. The key points are: 1. The case is remanded for further proceedings before the same court, with a new composition of judges excluding those from the previous ruling. 2. The unqualified employee (αναιρεσίβλητη) is ordered to pay 500 euros to the qualified employee (αναιρεσείουσα) as part of the legal costs. 3. The decision was rendered in Athens on January 16, 2013 and published publicly at a session in Athens on February 10, 2014. 4. The Vice President and Secretary General signed the decision. The main reasons for this ruling were: - The Ephetae's interpretation and application of relevant provisions of the Collective Labor Agreement (ΣΣΕ) was incorrect. - They failed to consider whether the earnings from the unqualified employee's work exceeded or did not exceed the requested payment amount on a monthly basis. - This constituted an illegal omission under Article 559 para.1

This decision addresses an appeal against a judgment by the Athens Epheta (a Greek court of first instance) regarding a dispute between two parties over payment for services. The key points are: 1. The Epheta's original decision was annulled in part, specifically the portion that incorrectly interpreted and applied Articles 3 and 4(4) of Law 166/2003. 2. The case is being referred back to the same court for further examination by a different panel of judges. 3. The party that appealed (the "enagoussa" or seller) will be reimbursed for their legal costs, set at 3,000 euros. 4. The other party (the "anairesiousa" or buyer) was found liable for these legal fees. 5. The decision is dated December 17, 2013 and published on February 11, 2014 in an open session of the court. 6. The case involves a dispute over contracts for services between a private company and a public authority, specifically regarding the calculation of overdue payment interest. The key legal points relate to how Articles 3 and 4(4) of Law 166/2003

The summary of the case is as follows: - The appeal by K. P. against A. P.'s decision (case no. 1862/2012) was rejected. - The complaint was ordered to be submitted to the Public Treasury. - The person who filed the appeal (K. P.) was fined 2,700 euros for filing an unfounded appeal. This decision was rendered in Athens on January 7, 2014 and published publicly at a session of the court held on February 5, 2014. The Vice President and Secretary also signed this decision.

Here's a summary of the key points: - The case concerns an appeal against an earlier decision by the Athens Polytechnic Court. - The main issue is whether the court properly considered claims for wages and leave entitlements from 2001 in its 2013 decision, despite not explicitly mentioning them. - The Higher Administrative Court (Πολυτεχνικό Πρωτεύουσα) found that: - The Polytechnic Court had indeed considered these claims implicitly - There was no other reason to overturn the original decision - Therefore, the appeal was rejected and the former employees were ordered to pay reduced legal costs (300 euros). - The decision was made in Athens on December 13, 2013 and published publicly on January 24, 2014. The summary focuses on the key findings and outcome of the case as presented.

This decision involves a legal case where an individual (D. Syz. L. K., of the gender D. M.) appealed against a decision by the Ioannina Court of First Instance regarding two immovable properties (in Greek, "ένδικα ακίνητα"). The key points are: 1. The appeal was rejected. 2. The case is related to an earlier decision (66/2011) from the same court that ordered additional evidence for these properties but failed to cite a previous decision (387/2009). 3. This failure led to the rejection of the appeal under Article 559, paragraph 19 and 15 of the Greek Civil Procedure Code. 4. The case is now referred to the state treasury due to the unsuccessful appeal. 5. The appellant will be fined 2700 euros for legal costs. The decision was rendered in Athens on December 16, 2013, and published publicly at a session of the court in Athens on January 22, 2014.

The summary of the judgment is as follows: - The appeal to overturn the decision by the Athens Court of First Instance (Case No. 2934/2012) was successful. - The case will be referred back to the same court, but with a different panel of judges for further investigation. - The costs are to be shared between both parties due to their joint victory and defeat. The judgment was rendered in Athens on November 12, 2013, and announced publicly at an open session in the courthouse on January 9, 2014.

This appears to be a legal decision from the Athens Court of Appeals regarding an appeal against a previous court decision in Kerkyra (Corfu). The key points are: 1. The case involves two parties who were not present for the initial hearing on December 5, 2012. 2. Only one party (ΙΚΑ ΤΕΑΜ) was represented by a fully empowered attorney at the appeal hearing on December 16, 2013. 3. The other party did not appear or provide any representation for themselves or their representative. 4. Based on this absence and lack of proper representation for all parties, the court declared the entire discussion of the appeal request as invalid. 5. As a result, the decision states that the appeal is considered invalid for all parties involved. 6. The final decision was made in Athens and published publicly at an open session on January 8, 2014. The main reason given is that without proper representation from all parties, the court cannot properly hear or consider the case. This decision effectively dismisses the appeal due to procedural issues with party representation.

Here's a summary of the key points: - The case concerns an appeal against a decision by the Athens Ephetae court (a lower-level administrative court) regarding land expropriation in Attica, Greece. - The main arguments for annulment were: - Violation of Article 10(2') of Law 559/2016 - Improper acceptance of a claim affecting the outcome without proper evidence - Illegitimate extension of the contested area to include additional properties - The appeal court rejected these arguments and upheld the lower court's decision. - The plaintiff (Dionysos Municipality) was ordered to pay 600 euros in legal fees for each party they represented. - The final decision was made on December 5, 2013 in Athens. - It was publicly announced at a session of the court on January 8, 2014. - The Vice President and Secretary is named as signing off on it. In essence, this appears to be an administrative appeal case regarding land expropriation that was ultimately unsuccessful.

The case involves a decision by the Lamia Epheta (judicial body) regarding unpaid overtime and night work compensation for employees of a municipality. The key points are: 1. The Epheta initially ruled in favor of the employees, ordering the municipality to pay overdue overtime payments from January 12, 2007 to May 31, 2009. 2. The municipality appealed this decision, arguing that the nature of their employment relationship had been incorrectly classified as "dedicated" based on certain court rulings (Αρχηγός Παράδειγμα 328/2005 and 329/2005). 3. The Higher Epheta (Υψηλότερος Εφέτης) rejected this appeal for two reasons: - It found that the Epheta had correctly applied the relevant legal provisions based on existing court rulings. - The municipality's argument about the nature of employment relationship was not a valid ground for appeal. 4. As a result, the Higher Epheta upheld the original decision and ordered the municipality to pay the overdue compensation. 5. The municipality lost the case and was fined 6

This decision overturns a previous ruling by the Ioannina First Instance Court and the Ioannina High Court, and orders the following: 1. The defendant (referred to as "the debtor") must pay 6% interest per year on the overdue amount from the date of default. 2. The plaintiff (referred to as "the creditor") is awarded €1,500 for legal fees. 3. The decision applies to all parties involved in the case. The key points are: - The 6% interest rate is upheld. - The previous rulings that limited the interest rate were overturned. - The debtor must pay interest at 6% per year from the date of default. - Legal costs are awarded to the creditor.

This summary describes a court decision regarding the unlawful denial of an allowance to certain employees. Here are the key points: - The case was brought before the Polytechneio Protodikeio (High Court) in Athens. - The court ruled that the High Court's decision (6016/2012) denying the allowance to these employees was unlawful. - The case is referred back to the same court, but with different judges. - The dismissed individuals are fined 1500 euros each for their role in the dismissal process. - The decision was made on December 3, 2013, and announced publicly at a session of the court on December 18, 2013. The summary highlights that this case involves issues related to public sector employment law, specifically regarding allowances for certain employees. It also mentions violations of equal treatment principles as required by the Greek Constitution and relevant legislation.

This is a summary of an appellate court decision in Greece regarding a case about inheritance rights. Here are the key points: - The original ruling by the Epheta (a Greek court) was overturned. - The case will be sent back to the same Epheta, but with different judges. - The court ordered the person who was excluded from inheritance (referred to as "anairesitē" or "excluded one") to pay 3,000 euros in legal fees to those who were included in the inheritance. The decision was made on December 5, 2013, in Athens and published on December 16, 2013. The summary mentions that the court found the Epheta's ruling had improperly applied certain key laws related to inheritance rights, particularly regarding the rights of those excluded from an inheritance. The case involved a dispute over the estate of someone who died (Δ. Σ.), with one party seeking full inheritance rights as the only heirs and demanding payment from the excluded person for certain assets. The court found that the Epheta had incorrectly applied relevant laws in its decision, thus overturning it.

This decision rejects the appeal by the Greek State against a previous judgment, and includes several key points: 1. The court upholds the 2012 decision of the Dodecanese Court. 2. It rules that the Greek State's appeal should be rejected as unfounded. 3. The State is ordered to pay 300 euros in legal costs to the appellants. 4. The case was heard and decided in Athens on November 7, 2013. 5. The decision was publicly announced at a session of the court in Athens on December 16, 2013. The summary indicates that the Greek State's arguments were found insufficient to overturn the previous ruling, and it must now pay legal fees as part of the judgment.

This document appears to be a legal decision from the Greek Supreme Court (Areios Pagos) regarding an appeal against a previous court decision. Here's a summary of the key points: 1. The case concerns an appeal by the Hellenic Republic (Greek state) against a 2008 decision by the Larissa Regional Court. 2. The original case involved two parties: the Hellenic Republic and the Holy Monastery, which did not appear for the final hearing in 2012. 3. During the initial hearing in November 2011, only the state's representative appeared. The monastery's representative had previously expedited the proceedings by providing a copy of their appeal document to the opposing side. 4. In subsequent hearings in November 2012 and October 2013, only the state's representative appeared again, while the monastery did not. 5. The Supreme Court ruled that since the Holy Monastery had previously expedited the proceedings but did not appear for the final hearing, this constituted an improper acceleration of the case without proper representation. 6. Based on these findings, the Supreme Court declared the entire hearing process invalid and rejected the state's appeal against the 2008 decision. 7. The decision

This decision concerns an appeal against the dismissal of a public sector employee, specifically a guard at a museum. The key points are: 1. The employee was dismissed based on a 2012 decision by the Athens Ephoreia (local administrative body). 2. The case examined whether there had been any violations of constitutional or legal rights in the original dismissal process. 3. The court found that: - There were no violations of fundamental rights under Articles 4, 22(2) and 103(7,8) of the Greek Constitution. - The continuous employment relationship before April 18, 2001 could be considered as a single indefinite contract for the purposes of Article 8 of Law 2112/1999. 4. The court ruled that there was no valid reason to dismiss the employee under Article 559(1) KΠολΔ (the sole legal ground). 5. As such, it rejected the appeal and condemned the dismissal, ordering the employer to pay 300 euros in legal fees to the dismissed employee. 6. The decision was rendered on November 19, 2013, and published publicly at an Athens court session on December
